data.title: Resmed Inc reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Resmed Inc reported quarterly adjusted earnings of $2.55​​ per share for the quarter ended June 30, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of $2.08. The mean expectation of fifteen analysts for the quarter was for earnings of $2.48 per share. Wall Street expected results to range from $2.35 to $2.60 per share.

  • Revenue rose 10.2% to $1.35 billion from a year ago; analysts expected $1.32 billion.

  • Resmed Inc's reported EPS for the quarter was $2.58​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $379.7 million.

  • Resmed Inc shares had risen by 7.5% this quarter and gained 21.3% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 0.8% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 13 "strong buy" or "buy," 5 "hold" and 1 "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the advanced medical equipment & technology peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Resmed Inc is $276.66, about 1.7% below its last closing price of $271.94

data.title: Wells Fargo to Name CEO Charlie Scharf Chairman — Update
data.description:
By Colin Kellaher

Wells Fargo said it plans to name Chief Executive Charlie Scharf to the additional post of chairman, rewarding the executive who led the bank back from a scandal involving the creation of millions of fake customer accounts.

Wells Fargo on Thursday said its board has also awarded Scharf a one-time special equity grant consisting of $30 million in restricted share rights and nearly 1.05 million stock options.

Wells Fargo said the actions reflect the board's desire to keep Scharf as CEO, a post he has held since joining in 2019, and to recognize his leadership in transforming the San Francisco bank as it worked to repair its reputation after the fake-accounts scandal erupted nearly a decade ago.

Scharf's efforts led to the termination of 13 consent orders during his tenure, culminating in last month's decision by the Federal Reserve to lift a restriction that capped the fourth-largest U.S. bank's assets at $2 trillion.

For the first time in seven years, Wells Fargo will be able to grow its balance sheet and redirect the resources it had been pouring into efforts to fix itself.

Steven Black, a Wells Fargo board member since April 2020, has been independent chairman since August 2021. The bank said the board plans to name a lead independent director to support its continued independent oversight after Scharf takes on the chairman post.

-Gina Heeb contributed to this article.

Write to Colin Kellaher at colin.kellaher@wsj.com

data.title: MicroStrategy just posted a huge profit. It's the second time riding on an accounting rule change.
data.description:
By Claudia Assis

Wall Street expected a modest quarterly loss of the bitcoin holder

MicroStrategy Inc., the enterprise-software company that calls itself the largest corporate holder of cryptocurrency, posted a massive quarterly profit late Thursday, thanks to a recent accounting rule change and a recent rally for bitcoin.

MicroStrategy (MSTR), which does business as Strategy, said that its operating income ballooned to $14.03 billion, an year-on-year increase of more than 7,000% as it included an unrealized gain on the company's digital assets of $14 billion, which MicroStrategy was allowed to count because of the accounting change.

The stock rose a little over 1% in the extended session Thursday, as it was the second quarter that MicroStrategy was allowed to factor in the unrealized gains on its digital assets.

The company reported net income of $10.02 billion, or $32.60 a share, versus a net loss of $102.6 million, or 57 cents a share, in the second quarter of 2024.

Analysts polled by FactSet modeled a second-quarter GAAP loss of 10 cents a share.

MicroStrategy guided for full-year operating income of $34 billion, net income of $24 billion and earnings of $80 a share, based on a bitcoin price outlook of $150,000 by the end of the year, it said.

Analysts polled by FactSet expect a GAAP loss of $16.32 a share for the year.

The company said it holds 628,791 bitcoins (BTCUSD), at a total cost of $46.07 billion, or $73,277 per token year to date.

The company recently passed on buying more bitcoin, the first time it had done so in three months. That led the lone analyst bearish on the company to make a case for selling the stock.

MicroStrategy began amassing its bitcoin fortress in 2020 as a defense against inflation.

The stock is up about 39% so far this year, compared with gains of around 8% for the S&P 500 index SPX.

-Claudia Assis

data.title: Geico, Berkshire Hathaway's Car Insurer, Is on Recovery Road — WSJ
data.description:
By Heather Gillers

Geico's comeback in the car-insurance wars is incomplete.

The Berkshire Hathaway-owned company, known for its green gecko mascot, has pared staff, invested in technology and tweaked pricing models in ways that have made it a more profitable underwriter.

The next challenge is growth. The company, which Warren Buffett has called his "first business love," is losing ground to rival Progressive in the share of Americans' total car-insurance dollars it collects. Both companies trail industry leader State Farm.

Investors will get a peek at Geico's progress Saturday, when Berkshire releases quarterly results. Analysts expect it will report around 10% growth in premiums written, up from 7% in the earlier quarter. Progressive posted 17% growth across its insurance lines for the first quarter — citing personal auto insurance as a crucial driver — and 12% in the second.

"I don't think anyone is going to top Progressive year-to-date," CFRA Research analyst Cathy Seifert said. "But I don't necessarily think the market is expecting Geico to become the next Progressive."

Geico had once been the powerhouse, holding the No. 2 spot in market share behind State Farm. But in recent years, Progressive caught up and then surpassed Geico by using its superior technology and data-crunching skills to pick out the best drivers and charge them the right prices.

Geico was weighed down by less-profitable customers accumulated during a decade-ago advertising blitz, when the firm's pricing skills weren't as sharp. Over the past several years, profits rebounded as the firm honed its technology and analytics, pulled back on advertising and reduced staff.

The test for Geico now is whether the firm can add policyholders without sacrificing those profits, said Brian Sullivan, editor of the trade newsletter Auto Insurance Report.

"They say they are going to grow and we just have to wait and see if it happens," he said. "If their prices are more accurate than their competitors', then a low-risk customer will get a good deal and buy, and a high-risk customer will get a bad deal and not buy."

Policyholders are ready for the gecko's money-saving pitch. After years of double-digit rate hikes, they have been comparison-shopping at record levels. Almost half of drivers sought quotes from other insurers in 2024, LexisNexis Risk Solutions found.

The timing works for Buffett, too. He no doubt wants to leave the company, one of his favorites, in the best shape possible when he steps down as CEO of Berkshire in December. And he has long singled out Progressive as the firm to beat. "It's a two-horse race," Buffett once told shareholders.

But Progressive and its excitable salesperson Flo lapped Geico and its gecko in that race a while ago.

One example is usage-based insurance, in which in-car devices and smartphone apps collect information on driving habits such as braking and speeding. Progressive pioneered the tool and has been offering it to policyholders since at least 2008. Geico started in 2019. Buffett told The Wall Street Journal in 2014 he didn't see the evidence for it.

Most Americans still don't use such tracking. But it enables insurers to offer better prices to safer drivers whom they couldn't necessarily identify based on traditional factors such as age, gender and car type.

"If you are not using that tool, you are going to lose your best people to Progressive and you don't even know they are your best people," said Meyer Shields, a managing director at Keefe, Bruyette & Woods.

Geico's other technology lagged behind as well. As recently as two years ago, the firm had "more than 600 legacy systems that don't really talk to each other," said Berkshire insurance chief Ajit Jain. All that put Progressive in a better position to figure out the risk — and the potential cost — of each policyholder, the science that is the bedrock of the insurance business. Even Geico's trademark low-overhead strategy of selling policies by phone and online without agents only got the company so far.

It was a problem Buffett had been warned about more than a decade earlier. One of his top portfolio managers had worked at Progressive in his 20s and told Buffett that it was a better company around the time he joined Berkshire in 2010.

"I said Geico's better at marketing and branding, but Progressive is a data company and data is going to win in the long run," the manager, Todd Combs, recounted on a podcast two years ago.

In January 2020, after years of trailing Progressive in a significant measure of underwriting profitability — the percentage of premiums going to claims and overhead — Buffett installed Combs as the firm's chief executive. Combs cut the number of employees by one-third, to 28,000, and hired new data analysts. He took a pause on trying to grow Geico, reducing advertising spending.

By 2023, the company was beating Progressive by that measure, and Geico's underwriting earnings had climbed higher than those of Berkshire's other insurers and reinsurers combined.

Buffett in February called the improvement "spectacular" but "not yet complete." A few months later, at this year's annual meeting, Jain congratulated Geico on "a great job."

"We've achieved a lot," he said. "Todd has achieved a lot. But I do not want to be so arrogant as to say that mission accomplished."

Write to Heather Gillers at heather.gillers@wsj.com

data.title: Australia house prices keep climbing but growth pace slows
data.description:
Australian home prices extended their steady rise to hit another record high in July as low housing supply boosted auction clearance rates, while expectations of lower rate cuts ahead are set to lift prices more this year, data showed on Friday.

Figures from property consultant Cotality showed national home prices rose 0.6% to A$844,197 ($542,397) in July, the sixth straight month of gains. That compared with a 0.6% rise in June and brought the annual growth to 3.7%.

"The outlook for housing values remains positive," Cotality's Research Director Tim Lawless said in a note.

"We expect values to continue posting a broad-based but modest rise through the rest of the year, supported by an outlook for lower interest rates, improving sentiment and short housing supply."

But he said the pace of growth "is no longer accelerating" as prices run up against stretched valuations, affordability constraints and lingering uncertainty.

Every major city recorded a price increase through the month, led by Darwin with a 2.2% rise and followed by Perth, up 0.9%. Sydney clocked a 0.6% rise lifting the median house prices in Australia's largest city to A$1.23 million ($790,275).

That should trend upwards through the year after data showed on Wednesday that Australian consumer prices grew at the slowest pace in over four years in the June quarter. Core inflation now sits firmly around the mid-point of the central bank's range and cemented market wagers for a cut in interest rates this month.

Lawless said consumer sentiment looked more positive as cost-of-living pressures ease and the cash rate moves lower.

"Historically, consumer sentiment and housing activity have shown a close relationship," he added.

($1 = 1.5564 Australian dollars)

data.title: Ray Dalio sells remaining stake in Bridgewater, letter says
data.description:
Investor Ray Dalio sold his remaining stake in Bridgewater Associates, the hedge fund he founded 50 years ago, according to a letter sent to the fund's investors and seen by Reuters.

"We wanted to update you that Bridgewater recently repurchased the last remaining ownership shares held by Dalio-related entities," Bridgewater's Chief Executive Officer Nir Bar Dea said in the letter.

The Wall Street Journal first reported Dalio's sale of his stake in Bridgewater.

Dalio had handed over control of Bridgewater to a new generation of investors in 2022.

He did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment about the sale.

data.title: iRhythm Technologies, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
iRhythm Technologies, Inc., a leading digital healthcare company specializing in the development and commercialization of advanced cardiac monitoring solutions, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report highlights significant improvements in both financial and operational performance, driven by increased revenue and enhanced operational efficiency.

Financial Highlights

Revenue, net: iRhythm Technologies reported net revenue of $186.7 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, marking a substantial increase from $148.0 million in the same period of 2024. This growth was primarily driven by higher sales to contracted third-party payors and healthcare institutions.

Gross Profit: The company's gross profit rose to $132.9 million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, up from $103.5 million in the same period of 2024. This improvement reflects better cost management and operational efficiency.

Loss from operations: iRhythm Technologies reduced its loss from operations to $(18.7) million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(23.0) million in the same period of 2024, demonstrating a reduction in operating losses due to increased revenue.

Net loss: The company reported a net loss of $(14.2) million for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(20.1) million in the same period of 2024, indicating improved financial performance.

Net loss per common share, basic and diluted: The net loss per common share, basic and diluted, was $(0.44) for the three months ended June 30, 2025, compared to $(0.65) in the same period of 2024, reflecting a reduction in losses per share.

Business Highlights

Revenue Segments: The company derives its revenue primarily from contracted third-party payors, which accounted for 52% of revenue in the three and six months ended June 30, 2025. Centers for Medicare and Medicaid contributed 24% of revenue, while healthcare institutions and non-contracted third-party payors accounted for 17% and 7% of revenue, respectively.

Geographical Performance: Revenue generated from the United States comprised substantially all of the company's revenue, indicating a strong domestic market presence.

Operational Performance: iRhythm Technologies has provided its iRhythm Services to over eight million patients, collecting over 2 billion hours of curated heartbeat data. This showcases the company's extensive operational scale and data collection capabilities.

Future Outlook: The company is focused on expanding its development program into other clinical-grade wearables to detect and characterize arrhythmias while integrating with clinicians' workflows. This indicates a strategic direction towards innovation and product diversification.

SEC Filing: iRhythm Technologies, Inc. [ IRTC ] - 10-Q - Jul. 31, 2025

data.title: Paramount Posts Mixed Results After Trump Settlement and Skydance Agreement — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Paramount Global earnings are on deck for Thursday afternoon after the media and entertainment company has spent several weeks in the political spotlight.

Paramount is scheduled to report second-quarter financial results after the stock market closes. Analysts surveyed by FactSet expect the company to post adjusted earnings of 36 cents a share on revenue of $6.87 billion.

In the same period last year, Paramount reported earnings of 54 cents a share on revenue of $6.81 billion.

Wall Street predicts Paramount's steaming service, Paramount+, had 77.5 million global subscribers in the second quarter, which would be a drop from last quarter's 79 million. Direct-to-consumer revenue is expected to be $2.1 billion, compared to last year's $1.88 billion. TV media revenue is expected to drop to $4.01 billion from $4.27 billion last year, as the traditional TV advertising market remains under pressure.

Even if Paramount reports knockout second-quarter financials, investors are most likely much more interested in commentary the company has to share regarding recent headlines involving Paramount, President Donald Trump, Stephen Colbert, Skydance, and South Park.

Trump sued CBS — one of Paramount's TV channels — in 2024, alleging the network deceitfully edited a 60 Minutes interview with then-Vice President Kamala Harris. Paramount and Trump settled the lawsuit in early July, with Paramount agreeing to pay Trump $16 million. Critics of the controversial settlement — which Trump said on Truth Social was a " big and important win," while Paramount did not apologize — pointed out that the deal happened while Paramount was waiting for the Federal Communications Commission to approve its highly-anticipated merger with Skydance.

The FCC ultimately approved the merger between Paramount and Skydance last week. Paramount, which expects the deal to close on Aug. 7, will be known as Paramount Skydance Corporation, and the company will use the new stock ticker PSKY.

Investors, however, may be taking stock of the many events that happened between the settlement and the merger's approval.

Less than a week before the FCC signed off on the merger, CBS announced that it was canceling The Late Show With Stephen Colbert. The move generated national attention, as critics pointed out that the cancelation came days after Colbert called the $16 million settlement a " big fat bribe." CBS has said canceling The Late Show was purely a financial decision.

Three days after the talk show was canceled, Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D.-Mass.), Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), and Sen. Ron Wyden (D-Or.) sent a letter to Skydance CEO David Ellison that said "it is illegal to corruptly give anything of value to federal officials to influence an official act — and if Paramount settles the lawsuit in a quid-pro-quo arrangement to influence the Administration's assessment of the Paramount-Skydance deal, company officials could be breaking the law."

Barron's has reached out to Paramount and Skydance for comment.

One day before the FCC signed off on the merger, Comedy Central — a channel owned by Paramount — and the creators of South Park, one of the most popular shows on the channel — agreed to a five-year deal that was reportedly worth $1.5 billion. The South Park contract was announced the same day the show's 27 season premiere episode aired, which included commentary about the Colbert cancellation and crude jokes regarding Trump.

Seaport analyst David Joyce downgraded shares of Paramount to Sell from Neutral on Monday, with an $11 price target.

"There are questions surrounding the go-forward strategy with the company eliminating one popular show ( The Late Show with Stephen Colbert), [and] paying a robust level for another ( South Park) where negotiations had been contentious," Joyce wrote.

Shares of Paramount were down 6.7% to $12.41 in afternoon trading on Thursday. The stock has risen 19% this year.

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

data.title: INNODATA INC SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Innodata Inc., a leading provider of digital data solutions and AI services, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter, showcasing significant improvements in both financial and operational metrics. The company's robust performance is driven by growth in its Digital Data Solutions (DDS) segment and expanding capabilities in AI data preparation and model deployment.

Financial Highlights

Innodata Inc. reported impressive financial results for the third quarter:

  • Revenues: $58.393 million, reflecting a significant increase from $32.553 million in the same period last year, driven by growth in the DDS segment.
  • Gross Profit: $23.023 million, up from $9.351 million, indicating improved efficiency and cost management.
  • Income before provision for income taxes: $9.488 million, a substantial increase compared to $0.276 million, highlighting strong operational performance.
  • Net Income (loss) attributable to Innodata Inc. and Subsidiaries: $7.219 million, compared to a loss of $0.014 million, demonstrating a turnaround in profitability.
  • Diluted EPS: $0.20, compared to $(0.00), reflecting improved earnings per share due to increased net income.

Business Highlights

Innodata Inc. also reported notable operational achievements and strategic initiatives:

  • Revenue Segments: The Digital Data Solutions (DDS) segment generated significant revenue, with a notable increase in its contribution to the company's total revenue. The Synodex and Agility segments also contributed to the overall revenue, with Agility showing a stable performance.
  • Geographical Performance: The United States remains the largest market, contributing the majority of the company's revenue. Other significant markets include Canada, the United Kingdom, and the Netherlands, which also showed strong performance.
  • AI Data Preparation: The company supports large technology companies in building generative AI foundation models, providing scaled data solutions such as instruction data sets for fine-tuning large language models and reinforcement learning services.
  • AI Model Deployment and Integration: Innodata helps businesses leverage AI technologies by developing custom AI models and fine-tuning proprietary and third-party models for domain-specific use cases.
  • Future Outlook: The company is focused on expanding its capabilities in AI data preparation and model deployment, aiming to enhance its position in the generative AI services market. Management anticipates continued growth in these areas, driven by increasing demand for high-quality data and AI solutions.

data.title: Heard on the Street Recap: Tech Bonanza — WSJ
data.description:
By Heard Editors

What Happened in Markets Today

Microsoft and Meta rose after blockbuster quarterly earnings driven by AI. Microsoft ended the session up 4% and ended trading with a market capitalization near $4 trillion. Meta's solid results and sunny outlook sent its shares 11% higher.

Copper plummeted. U.S. copper futures fell more than 22%-their worst daily performance on record. The plunge followed President Trump imposition of 50% tariff on imports of copper products including wire and pipe, rather than raw copper as the market had been expecting.

U.S. tariffs on Mexico were extended. The U.S. will keep tariffs on Mexican goods in place for 90 days as the countries continue to discuss a trade deal, Trump said, citing the "complexities" of making a deal with the U.S.'s southern neighbor.

Core U.S. inflation rose in June. The Fed's closely watched inflation gauge, called core PCE, picked up to 2.8% from a year earlier, new data showed. Prices rose at a slightly quicker pace than in May.

Market indexes fell slightly. The tech-heavy Nasdaq was flat, despite positive earnings reports. The S&P 500 fell 0.4% and the Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 330 points.

This analysis comes from the Journal's Heard on the Street team. Subscribe to their free daily afternoon newsletter here.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Cactus, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Cactus, Inc., a leading provider of wellhead and pressure control equipment, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial and operational performance, highlighting key metrics and business activities that have shaped its recent performance.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenues: $553.9 million, reflecting a slight decrease of 1.9% compared to the same period in 2024 due to reduced customer activity levels.
  • Operating Income: $129.4 million, a decrease of 9.1% from the first six months of 2024, primarily due to increased legal expenses and tariff impacts.
  • Net Income: $103.2 million, down 8.6% from the same period in 2024, influenced by lower operating income and increased working capital requirements.
  • Earnings per Class A share - diluted: $1.23, compared to $1.35 in the same period of 2024, reflecting the decrease in net income.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The Pressure Control segment generated $370.0 million in revenue for the first six months of 2025, reflecting a 2.2% increase from the same period in 2024, driven by enhanced customer drilling efficiencies. The Spoolable Technologies segment reported $188.8 million in revenue, a 6.9% decrease due to reduced customer activity levels.
  • Geographical Performance: The company operates predominantly in the U.S. onshore market, with additional operations in Australia, Canada, and the Middle East. The Pressure Control segment supports its operations through service centers in key oil and gas producing regions in the U.S. and Australia, while the Spoolable Technologies segment has service centers and pipe yards in the U.S. and Western Canada.
  • Sales Units: The Pressure Control segment's demand is primarily driven by the number of new wells drilled, as each new well requires a wellhead and a production tree. The Spoolable Technologies segment's demand is driven by the number of wells being placed into production, as customers use spoolable pipe and fittings to bring wells onto production.
  • Future Outlook: The company anticipates that its existing cash on hand, cash generated from operations, and available borrowings will be sufficient to meet its short-term cash requirements, including the cash purchase price related to the Baker Hughes Transaction. The company is also evaluating the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which is expected to lead to accelerated tax deductions.

SEC Filing: Cactus, Inc. [ WHD ] - 10-Q - Jul. 31, 2025

data.title: Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Boot Barn Holdings, Inc., the largest lifestyle retail chain in the U.S. focused on western and work-related footwear, apparel, and accessories, has released its Form 10-Q report for the second quarter of fiscal 2025. The report highlights significant growth in both financial and operational metrics, underscoring the company's robust market position and strategic expansion efforts.

Financial Highlights

Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. reported impressive financial results for the second quarter:

  • Net sales: $504.1 million, a 19.1% increase from the previous year, driven by incremental sales from new stores and a 9.4% increase in consolidated same store sales.
  • Gross profit: $197.2 million, up 25.8% from the previous year, with a gross profit margin improvement of 210 basis points to 39.1%, primarily due to better buying economies of scale, lower freight expense, and growth in exclusive brand penetration.
  • Income from operations: $70.7 million, a 40.8% increase from the previous year, reflecting improved sales and cost efficiencies.
  • Net income: $53.4 million, up from $38.9 million in the previous year, attributed to higher sales and improved gross profit margins.
  • Net income as a percentage of Net sales: 10.6%, up from 9.2% in the previous year, indicating improved profitability.

Business Highlights

Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. also reported several key operational achievements and strategic initiatives:

  • Business Overview: Boot Barn operates 473 stores across 49 states as of June 28, 2025, making it the largest lifestyle retail chain in the U.S. focused on western and work-related footwear, apparel, and accessories.
  • Product Offering: The company's product range includes a wide selection of western and work boots, apparel, and accessories, catering to both western enthusiasts and workers seeking high-quality footwear and apparel.
  • Store Operations: Boot Barn's stores offer a comprehensive assortment of brands and styles, supported by knowledgeable store associates, aiming to provide an authentic, one-stop shopping experience.
  • Customer Demographics: The company targets a broad demographic, including western and country enthusiasts and workers, with a focus on utility and brand-driven purchases.
  • Same Store Sales: Same store sales increased by 9.4% for the thirteen weeks ended June 28, 2025, driven by a 9.3% increase in e-commerce same store sales and a 9.5% increase excluding e-commerce.
  • New Store Openings: The company continues to expand its footprint, with new store openings contributing to incremental sales growth.
  • Operational Strategy: Boot Barn leverages its broad geographic footprint to achieve economies of scale, enhance supplier relationships, and reinvest in its business, maintaining a competitive edge over its nearest competitors.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue opening new stores, remodeling existing ones, and investing in distribution centers and e-commerce infrastructure, with estimated capital expenditures for fiscal 2026 between $115.0 million and $120.0 million.

SEC Filing: Boot Barn Holdings, Inc. [ BOOT ] - 10-Q - Jul. 31, 2025

data.title: Uber says some sexual assault accusers submitted fake receipts
data.description:
Key points:
  • Uber says receipts contained factual, formatting errors
  • Passengers' lawyers reviewing Uber's claims
  • Uber faces thousands of passenger lawsuits

Uber said it found more than 100 instances in which passengers who claimed its drivers sexually assaulted or harassed them offered bogus or doctored receipts to prove ridership, or did not explain their inability to provide receipts.

In a Wednesday court filing, Uber urged U.S. District Judge Charles Breyer in San Francisco to order 21 plaintiffs with suspect receipts to justify why their claims should not be dismissed, and 90 plaintiffs with no receipts to either provide them or "non-boilerplate" reasons for their absence.

At least 11 law firms represent the various plaintiffs, court papers show. They were not accused of wrongdoing.

Uber is trying to reduce its liability in nationwide federal litigation comprising more than 2,450 lawsuits alleging driver misconduct. The San Francisco-based company faces several hundred additional lawsuits in San Francisco Superior Court.

“We take any allegations seriously and are reviewing Uber's latest claims," lawyers Rachel Abrams, Sarah London and Roopal Luhana, who lead the federal litigation on behalf of the plaintiffs, said in a joint statement.

They added that Uber knows some sexual assault victims may lack receipts because other people ordered their rides, and the possible elevated risk of being assaulted that guest passengers face makes Uber's focus on documentation "more troubling."

Uber has maintained it should not be liable for criminal conduct by drivers it connects with passengers, and that its background checks and disclosures were sufficient.

On July 8, Breyer dismissed some fraud and liability claims that were based on ads promoting Uber's ride-sharing service as a safe alternative to drunk driving.

In Wednesday's filing, Uber said some fake receipts appear to have been generated through third-party websites.

Uber said some receipts contained math errors or bogus surcharges, changed female driver names to male names, were time-stamped before rides occurred, had stray marks, or used formatting that does not match its own.

One plaintiff submitted two receipts for a single ride, while two plaintiffs submitted different versions of the same receipt, the company said.

"Nothing is more critical to the integrity of our judicial system than honesty," Uber said. "It is difficult to conceive an act of misconduct graver than the outright fabrication of evidence that plaintiffs here undertook."

The case is In re Uber Technologies Inc Passenger Sexual Assault Litigation, U.S. District Court, Northern District of California, No. 23-03084.

data.title: Ballard Power Systems to Begin Cost-Saving Realignment
data.description:
By Nicholas G. Miller

Ballad Power Systems will begin a cost-saving realignment intended to achieve positive cash flow by the end of 2027.

The fuel cell manufacturer said it would reduce operating costs by 30% in 2026 through workforce adjustments and streamlined operations.

It also said it would discontinue costly and low-margin fuel cell programs and limit capital expenditures.

Ballad Power Systems had a first-quarter loss from continuing operations of 7 cents a share on revenue of $15.4 million. The company will report its second-quarter earnings on August 11.

Write to Nicholas G. Miller at nicholas.miller@wsj.com.

data.title: UnitedHealth appoints Wayne DeVeydt as CFO in another management shake-up
data.description:
UnitedHealth said on Thursday it will replace its finance chief John Rex with an external hire, Wayne DeVeydt, adding to the management changes at the health insurer that has been struggling to control its medical costs.

Rex, who has been handling the role since 2016, will continue as a strategic advisor to the CEO, with the changes effective September 2.

DeVeydt was most recently managing director and operating partner at Bain Capital, and was also the finance chief of UnitedHealth's rival Elevance , then called Anthem, between 2007 and 2016.

The largest U.S. health insurer had seen an abrupt departure of Andrew Witty as CEO in May, with Stephen Hemsley replacing him following struggles with elevated medical costs that were also seen in the rest of the industry.

Hemsley is under pressure to regain investor trust as the company faces financial struggles and reputational damage that surfaced after the then-CEO of its health insurance unit was gunned down on a New York City street in December.

data.title: GM Stock Rises. Why Trump's South Korea Trade Deal Is Good News. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

The U.S. has a trade deal with South Korea. The company breathing a sigh of relief on the news is General Motors.

It operates several facilities in the country, including two assembly plants.

GM stock rose 2.4% on Thursday, closing at $53.34, while the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 0.4% and 0.7%, respectively.

"I am pleased to announce that the United States of America has agreed to a Full and Complete Trade Deal with the Republic of Korea," wrote President Donald Trump on Truth Social Wednesday evening. "We have agreed to a Tariff for South Korea of 15%. America will not be charged a Tariff." The deal also includes South Korean investment in the U.S. and the purchase of U.S. energy products.

It's good news for General Motors, which imports cars from its plants in South Korea. Before the deal, the country faced 25% sectoral tariffs on imported cars.

"GM appreciates that negotiators have reached an agreement on trade between the US and South Korea," the company told Barron's in an emailed statement. "GM's long-standing operations in Korea produce high-quality crossovers that complement our U.S. vehicles, strengthening GM's global competitiveness and benefitting American customers and our U.S. operations."

In 2024, GM imported roughly 45% of the cars it sold domestically, mainly from Mexico and South Korea. That ratio is changing. In June, GM announced $4 billion in U.S. investments that will ultimately shift some production back in to the U.S. GM's Spring Hill Manufacturing in Tennessee, for instance, will add the Chevy Blazer in 2027. Today, the Blazer is assembled in Mexico.

GM estimated a 2025 tariff impact of about $4.5 billion, before any efforts by the company to offset rising costs. That number will likely be lower with the new deal. Investors now await news on tariff rates with Canada and Mexico.

The South Korean trade deal comes just after Ford Motor reported second-quarter earnings that included some $800 million in increased tariff-related costs. Ford management raised their estimate for 2025 tariff impacts to about $2 billion from $1.5 billion. Ford CFO Sherry House pointed out that increased tariffs on steel and aluminum, which raised prices for domestic materials, were partly to blame for the increase.

Ford management also said that 15% tariff rates, which are part of trade deals with Japan, the EU, and South Korea, don't create a significant incentive to move manufacturing back to the U.S.

Even at a lower 15% rate, however, the tariffs represent an opportunity for Ford, said CEO Jim Farley on Ford's second-quarter earnings conference call on Wednesday evening. "Yes, it may be less than 25%. But I think we found with administration, they're really committed to companies like Ford, and they are going to work hard to reduce that liability," added Farley.

Coming into Thursday trading, Ford stock was up about 3% since the Nov. 5 election. GM stock was down about 3%. GM stock was down about 2% since the trade deal was announced with Japan about a week ago. Ford stock was down 4% over the same span.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com
